title: Population Growth
description: A healthy cohort of births strains food planning but promises a stronger next generation.

outcomes.label: Expand rations
outcomes.description: Feed the boom.
outcomes.log: The ship's population grew, and the galley budget grew with it.

outcomes.label: Hold rations steady
outcomes.description: No new allocations. The nursery decks will remember.
outcomes.log: Rations held steady through the birth cohort. Some decks kept score.

complications.description_add: And the larder is already thin — a birth cohort is a blessing the galley is in no position to feed.
complications.log: The new cohort arrived into a hungry year, and the galley stretched an already-thin larder thinner to feed them.

title: The Cache
description: A supply cache the mission planners seeded decades ago drifts on the course ahead, if its beacon can be trusted.

outcomes.label: Match and claim it
outcomes.description: Spend fuel to rendezvous with the drop.
outcomes.log: The cache gave up rations and metals — and, folded in its heart, an intact solar sail array, stowed in the salvage hold.

outcomes.label: Pass it by
outcomes.description: Stay on the fuel budget; the holds are full enough.
outcomes.log: The cache drifted past, unopened. The ship needs nothing yet, and the founders keep their gift.

outcomes.label: A fortune — sail home on it
outcomes.description: Far richer than charted. With holds full and a windfall aboard, the charter is as good as met.
outcomes.log: The cache was no mere resupply but a mother lode — metals and salvage worth more than the whole charter. The council turns the ship for home, holds heavy with fortune.

title: The Berth Lottery
description: This generation was born larger than the last, and there are more people than private berths. Someone decides who sleeps in the open decks.

outcomes.label: Expand the quarters
outcomes.description: Convert cargo and hold space into homes.
outcomes.log: Cargo space was cut open and rebuilt into berths. The ship carries less and shelters more.

outcomes.label: Run the lottery
outcomes.description: Assign the open decks by lot. Fair, and resented.
outcomes.log: Private berths were assigned by lottery. The ship called it fair, and the losers of the draw learned to sleep in the light.

outcomes.label: Let a people take the settlement offer
outcomes.description: A passing survey world will take a whole faction. Crowding eases; the ship is smaller for it.
outcomes.log: One people accepted a standing offer to make planetfall on a surveyed world. The berths are roomier now, and a light aboard has gone out for good.

title: The Garden World
description: A living world drifts across the route — blue water, breathable air, empty of people. It could refill the holds for a decade. Some aboard have already stopped calling it a stop.

outcomes.label: Resupply and go
outcomes.description: Fill the holds; keep the schedule.
outcomes.log: The ship gorged the world's bounty and pulled away before the temptation could set. The holds are full; the longing is not.

outcomes.label: Let the willing stay
outcomes.description: A world like this asks to be lived on.
outcomes.log: A whole people chose the garden world over the dark. The ship sailed lighter, and grieved the leaving even as it filled its holds.

title: The Skim
description: A gas giant sits astride the lane, its upper atmosphere thick with reaction mass. A skimming pass could top the tanks — if the hull can take the heat.

outcomes.label: Skim deep
outcomes.description: Dip low and fill the tanks.
outcomes.log: The ship dipped into the storm-lit clouds and came out streaked with soot and heavy with fuel.

outcomes.label: Skim shallow
outcomes.description: A cautious pass; take what's safe.
outcomes.log: A gentle skim gave the tanks a modest gift and the hull no new scars.

title: The Blight
description: A blight has taken the on-station crop and the seed vault with it. The mission's whole purpose here is rotting in the trays, and it will not come back this trip.

outcomes.label: Scour and replant
outcomes.description: Burn it out and try once more.
outcomes.log: The trays were scoured to bare metal and sown again from the last clean stock. It cost dearly, and it might yet hold.

outcomes.label: Cut losses and turn back
outcomes.description: There is nothing left to work for here.
outcomes.log: The blighted decks were sealed and the mission cut short. The ship came about, holds light and hopes lighter, for the long road home.

title: The Rationing Referendum
description: The stores are thin and the decks want a say in who eats. Impose the cuts from the council, or put rationing to the whole ship.

outcomes.label: Impose the cuts
outcomes.description: The council decides. Order over consent.
outcomes.log: The council rationed by decree. The stores held — but a whole quarter of the ship stopped calling the mission theirs, and one day a berth-block was simply empty.

outcomes.label: Put it to a vote
outcomes.description: Let the ship ration itself, unevenly and loudly.
outcomes.log: The ship voted on its own hunger. The math was worse and the peace was better.

title: The Green Temptation
description: The charter's route skims a living world, warm and unclaimed. A faction petitions to be let off here rather than sail on.

outcomes.label: Let them settle
outcomes.description: Fewer mouths, fewer hands, a colony you did not plan.
outcomes.log: A whole faction went down to the green world with seed and tools, and the ship sailed on lighter, quieter, and short a people.

outcomes.label: Hold the course
outcomes.description: The charter comes first. Sail past the temptation.
outcomes.log: The council refused the green world. The charter held, and for a generation people spoke of the paradise they were not allowed to keep.

title: The Living Decks
description: The Verdant Kin want to convert a cargo deck into gardens — more food, more air, more green — at the cost of the salvage bay the engineers rely on.

outcomes.label: Grow the gardens
outcomes.description: Trade the salvage bay for green.
outcomes.log: The cargo deck became a forest in miniature. The galley rejoiced and the machine shop mourned its lost bench space.

outcomes.label: Keep the salvage bay
outcomes.description: The ship runs on parts, not petals.
outcomes.log: The salvage bay stayed. The Kin tended window-boxes instead of a forest, and remembered being told no.

title: Rot in the Grow-Decks
description: A rot spreads through the hydroponic beds faster than the gardeners can cut it back. Burn the infected racks to save the rest, or nurse them and risk the whole harvest.

outcomes.label: Burn the infected racks
outcomes.description: Cut losses. Sacrifice a quarter to save three.
outcomes.log: The gardeners torched a quarter of the grow-decks and stopped the rot at the firebreak. The galley tightened its belt and the beds slowly greened again.

outcomes.label: Nurse the beds
outcomes.description: Try to save every rack. Gamble the harvest.
outcomes.log: The gardeners fought for every rack and lost most of them anyway. The rot burned through the decks and the ship went hungry into the next season.

title: The Dry Tank
description: The reaction-mass gauges read near-empty with the destination star still a lifetime ahead. Something has to give, or the ship coasts dark.

outcomes.label: Cut the drives and coast
outcomes.description: Shut down all but life support and fall toward home on momentum alone.
outcomes.log: The drives went cold and the ship coasted through the dark on nothing but momentum and nerve. It cost years and it cost comfort, but it kept flying.

outcomes.label: Feed the reactor the ship itself
outcomes.description: Break down non-essential structure for reaction mass.
outcomes.log: The engineers fed whole bulkheads into the reactor. The tank climbed off empty and the ship came home lighter, barer, and scarred by its own hunger.

title: The Lost Gardeners
description: The grow-decks yield less every cycle, and the reason is quiet: the master gardeners' craft — the reading of soil and light and blight — was never fully passed on. The beds are tended by rote, and the harvests know it.

outcomes.label: Apprentice a new generation of gardeners
outcomes.description: Set the young to the beds under what few masters remain.
outcomes.log: The last master gardeners took apprentices to the beds and taught the reading of soil and rot. The next harvest was thin; the ones after that were not.

outcomes.label: Automate the beds instead
outcomes.description: Replace craft with machinery and standing recipes.
outcomes.log: The beds were handed to machines and fixed recipes, and the gardener's craft became a museum piece. The yields steadied — brittle, blind, and wholly dependent on the machinery holding.

title: The Ice Moon
description: A frozen moonlet drifts near the lane, its crust thick with the volatiles that make reaction mass. Stopping to mine it costs time and labor the charter did not budget for.

outcomes.label: Stop and mine the ice
outcomes.description: Spend the weeks. Fill the tanks off the frozen moon.
outcomes.log: The ship anchored to the ice moon and melted its crust into the tanks. The stop cost a season and bought a decade of range.

outcomes.label: Burn on past it
outcomes.description: The charter waits for no moon. Keep the schedule.
outcomes.log: The navigators logged the ice moon and did not slow for it. The tanks stayed low and the schedule stayed honest, and someone noted the coordinates for a leaner day.

title: The Seed Corn
description: The stores are thin enough that the granary masters raise a forbidden option: eat the seed stock — the reserve meant to replant the grow-decks — and buy this generation's bellies against the next generation's harvests.

outcomes.label: Eat the seed corn
outcomes.description: Feed the living now. Gamble the future can be rebuilt.
outcomes.log: The granaries opened the seed vaults and the ship ate its own future. Bellies filled; the master gardeners wept over emptied reserves that had crossed light-years unbroken.

outcomes.label: Hold the seed stock
outcomes.description: The reserve is sacred. Bear the hunger.
outcomes.log: The council held the seed vaults shut and let the lean season take who it took. The grow-decks would replant; the graves would be remembered as the price of it.

title: The Barren Decks
description: A generation after the seed vaults were emptied, the grow-decks still yield thin and wrong — the strains that crossed the stars are gone, replaced by whatever could be scraped together. The ship tastes the old decision at every meal.

outcomes.label: Trade dearly for new seed
outcomes.description: Spend whatever it takes to restock the vaults properly.
outcomes.log: The ship spent a fortune on new seed stock from a passing convoy, and the grow-decks slowly greened into something worth eating again. The lesson stayed in the ledgers, underlined.

outcomes.label: Make do with what grows
outcomes.description: Adapt to the poorer strains. Save the credits.
outcomes.log: The ship made peace with its thin harvests and forgot, over a generation, that food had ever tasted otherwise. The seed corn was never mentioned; the flavor of the decision simply became normal.

title: The Long Winter
description: Thin stores and a browning reactor at once — the galley cannot cook what the fields cannot grow, and the cold is creeping into the habitat decks. A ship can survive hunger or dark for a while, but rarely both together.

outcomes.label: Ration hard and huddle close
outcomes.description: Cut portions, concentrate warmth, and endure it as one.
outcomes.log: The ship pulled its people into the warm decks and thinned every plate, and the long winter took its toll in the old and the unlucky. Those who came through it came through it together.

outcomes.label: Burn the reserves to break it
outcomes.description: Spend everything at once to end the crisis fast, cost be damned.
outcomes.log: The council threw the whole reserve at the long winter and broke it in a season, leaving the coffers bare and the ship warm. It was reckless and it worked, which is its own kind of tradition now.

complications.description_add: And this is a ship that forgot how to be hungry. The fat years raised a generation on full plates and lengthened feast-days, and now that the winter has come at last, they meet it with no thrift in the muscle and no memory of going without — the rationing bites a people who never learned to bear it.
complications.log: The long winter fell hardest on the generation the fat years had softened — they had never rationed, never gone cold and counted it ordinary, and the crossing took more of them than it should have, and took more heart besides. A ship pays for its plenty in the winter that follows it.

title: The Lean Pact
description: With the stores thin, the whole ship swears a rationing pact — equal shares, no exceptions, officers and infants alike. It is a hard, proud thing, and how it is remembered will shape the people for generations.

outcomes.label: Swear the pact ship-wide
outcomes.description: Equal shares for all, sealed by oath.
outcomes.log: The whole ship swore the lean pact, and for one hungry generation nobody ate a bite more than anyone else. It was remembered afterward as the year the crew became one people.

outcomes.label: Feed the essential crew first
outcomes.description: Keep the specialists strong; ration the rest harder.
outcomes.log: The council fed its specialists first and let the pact fail before it was sworn. The ship kept its experts sharp and its people divided into those who ate and those who watched.

title: The Drifting Tanker
description: A fuel tanker drifts dead on the lane, its crew long gone but its tanks — the sensors insist — still substantially full. Boarding a derelict is never free, but a low tank makes the risk look reasonable.

outcomes.label: Board and pump the tanks
outcomes.description: Cross to the derelict and take the reaction mass.
outcomes.log: The crews crossed to the dead tanker and pumped its tanks dry into their own. The ship came away fuelled for a decade and short a few who went aboard and did not radio back.

outcomes.label: Leave it drifting
outcomes.description: A dead ship's full tanks are a bad kind of luck. Pass it by.
outcomes.log: The navigators logged the drifting tanker and its too-convenient bounty and steered well clear. The old hands nodded; a full derelict is a story that rarely ends well.

title: The Overloaded Decks
description: The tow draws parasitically on the ship — its cold holds kept barely warm, its archives kept dry, all off your grid and your air. The strain is telling on life support. Bear the load, or let the dead ship go cold.

outcomes.label: Bear the load
outcomes.description: Keep the prize whole. Carry its weight on your own systems.
outcomes.log: The council chose to carry the tow warm and whole, and the habitat plant ran a little harder for it every year of the haul. The archives will reach home readable. The scrubbers will need answering for.

outcomes.label: Let it go cold
outcomes.description: Cut the derelict off your grid. Save the ship; lose what freezes.
outcomes.log: They pulled the tow off the grid and let it freeze. The ship's air came easier at once — and somewhere in the dead ship's dark holds, seed-stock and film and things that do not survive the cold quietly stopped being cargo.

title: The Fire and the Field
description: The First Flame keeps a shrine-burn lit off the reactor — an eternal fire that has not gone dark since launch. The Verdant Kin, short of light for the grow-decks, have asked for that power. Two faiths, one reactor, and only so many megawatts.

outcomes.label: Ration the reactor to the gardens
outcomes.description: Dim the shrine-burn and give the surplus to the grow-decks. The faithful will grumble; the gardens will grow.
outcomes.log: The shrine-burn was banked to a low steady glow and its spared power run to the grow-decks. The Flame's faithful watched their eternal fire dim and said nothing kind, and the gardens, for the first time in a generation, had light enough to spare.

outcomes.label: Keep the eternal fire at full burn
outcomes.description: The flame has not gone dark since launch and will not now. The gardens will make do.
outcomes.log: The eternal fire kept its full burn, and the faithful were honored, and the grow-decks stayed dim and thin. The Kin logged the harvest shortfall against the shrine and did not forget it, and the ship ate a little less to keep a fire lit.

title: The Broken Beds
description: The hydroponics decks have gone too long unmended — bed liners split, nutrient loops crusted shut, the circulation pumps seizing one by one. The gardens are dying faster than they can be picked, and a dying garden is a starving ship a few years on.

outcomes.label: Rebuild the hydroponics
outcomes.description: Spend the parts and the power to re-lay the beds and re-seat the pumps. Modern farming, kept alive.
outcomes.log: The grow-decks were torn out and re-laid, pump by pump, liner by liner, until the water ran clean and green again. It cost the ship a fortune in parts and power, and it bought back the one thing the ship cannot buy: next year's food.

outcomes.label: Fall back to soil farming
outcomes.description: Abandon the failed hydroponics for low-tech soil beds. Hardier, hungrier on space, and a lean few years while it takes.
outcomes.log: The ship gave up on the high hydroponics and turned to soil — dirt beds in every spare corridor, a way of farming the founders had thought left behind. It will feed the ship in time, but the lean years between broke some health that the crowded, hungry wards could not mend.

title: The Castaways
description: A distress beacon resolves into a crippled boat — the last of some other venture's people, generations adrift on failing air, with a hold of preserved stores they can no longer eat fast enough to matter. They ask to be taken aboard. Every berth given is another mouth for the rest of the crossing.

outcomes.label: Take them aboard
outcomes.description: Open the locks. More hands, more mouths, and a debt of mercy the ship will carry — and feed — the rest of the way.
outcomes.log: The locks opened and the castaways came aboard, gaunt and grateful, and the ship was suddenly larger and hungrier and a little more crowded in every corridor. The galley redid its arithmetic for the rest of the crossing, and no one aboard regretted the choice, exactly, though everyone felt it at the table.

outcomes.label: Take the stores, not the people
outcomes.description: Trade for their preserved cargo and leave them their boat. The ship cannot feed everyone who is starving in the dark.
outcomes.log: The ship took the castaways' stores at a fair rate and left them their boat and their heading, and pulled away from the beacon before it stopped transmitting. The larder was fuller for it. The crew that made the trade did not talk about it much, then or after.

title: The Windfall Harvest
description: The garden world beneath is more generous than the survey promised — a growing season the ship could gorge on for a decade. But there is far more here than the holds can carry, and only so long before the ship must break orbit. A glut is its own kind of problem.

outcomes.label: Preserve it for the long haul
outcomes.description: Dry, salt, and vault everything the holds will take. The crews will relearn the old craft of putting food up against a lean century.
outcomes.log: The whole ship turned to the harvest — picking, drying, salting, vaulting — until the holds groaned full and the crews had, between them, remembered how a people feeds itself across a barren stretch. The larder will outlast the memory of this world, and the craft of filling it was worth as much as the food.

outcomes.label: Take what stores, sell the rest forward
outcomes.description: Load the holds and log the surplus as a manifest to trade at the next port of call. Food now, credits later, and none of it put up for a crisis.
outcomes.log: The ship took its fill and struck a forward contract on the rest — a manifest of the windfall to be redeemed at a port a century downstream. The holds are full enough and the writ is worth a good sum, and if a lean year comes before the port does, well, the ledger looked handsome at the time.

title: The Ration Market
description: Rationing has done what rationing does: a quiet market has grown in the gaps of it, ration chits changing hands at three times face, the well-connected eating better than the fair share the council swore everyone would keep. The quartermasters want to know what the council intends to see.

outcomes.label: Break the market
outcomes.description: Raid the caches, void the hoarded chits, and make an example. Everyone back to the fair share, at the point of it if need be.
outcomes.log: Security walked the black market down in a week of hard searches and voided chits, and the fair share held again, resented and enforced. The ship ate equal and unhappy, and the ones who had been eating well remembered who had taken it from them.

outcomes.label: License it and tax it
outcomes.description: You cannot stop it, so make it pay. Legalize the trade, skim it for the common stores, and call the pragmatism a policy.
outcomes.log: The council licensed the ration market rather than fight it, taxed it for the common stores, and let a ship that was supposed to share alike learn to price its hunger instead. It kept the peace and paid a little into the tins, and it settled something about the ship that the founders had meant to be otherwise.

title: The Deep Stores
description: The first decades are the ship's slack years — holds fuller than they will ever be again, hands to spare. A quartermaster proposes spending some of that slack now: sealed deep-vaults of preserved food against a famine no one alive has yet seen, but every long voyage eventually does. The founders would call it prudence. The hungry-for-growth call it hoarding.

outcomes.label: Build the deep vaults
outcomes.description: Spend the early slack on sealed long-term stores. It is dead weight until the year it is everything.
outcomes.log: The ship gave its early abundance to the deep vaults — sealed holds of grain and dried protein tucked into the cold spine of the ship, meant to be forgotten until they were needed. It cost a fortune in slack the ship would rather have grown on, and it will be worth every gram the first time the gardens fail.

outcomes.label: Travel light
outcomes.description: Keep the slack for growth and flexibility. A ship that hoards against every fear never gets anywhere.
outcomes.log: The council kept the ship's early slack for living rather than storing, and the holds stayed lean and flexible and the decks a little more generous. It was the choice of a people who trusted the gardens, and the gardens had not yet given them reason not to.

title: The Vaults Answer
description: The gardens have failed and the ship is hungry — but generations ago, in the slack years, someone had the foresight to build the deep vaults, and they have waited in the cold spine of the ship for exactly this. The seals can be broken. The question is only how fast.

outcomes.label: Open the vaults
outcomes.description: This is the year they were built for. Break the seals and feed the ship.
outcomes.log: The deep vaults were opened, and grain the founders' founders had sealed away poured out onto the mess decks generations later, still good, still enough. The ship ate, and remembered a decision made by people none of them had known, and was grateful across the gap of years to hands long dead.

outcomes.label: Ration the reserve
outcomes.description: One famine is rarely the last. Draw on the vaults, but sparingly, and keep some sealed for a worse year.
outcomes.log: The council opened the vaults only a crack, drawing just enough to hold the ship through and sealing the rest back against a hunger yet to come. The decks grumbled at a thin ration atop a full larder, and the quartermasters slept better for a reserve kept whole.

title: The Slingshot Gambit
description: The tank is nearly dry and a massive body lies across the course. The navigators lay out a gambit: an unpowered slingshot, threading the ship close through the gravity well to steal velocity for nothing — no fuel spent, if it goes right. If it goes wrong, close is very close.

outcomes.label: Thread the well
outcomes.description: Take the free velocity and the risk that comes with it. Save the fuel for when there is no gravity to borrow.
outcomes.log: The ship threaded the gravity well close enough to frighten the founders' ghosts, stole a decade of velocity for not a gram of fuel, and came out the far side scorched along one flank and short a dozen souls who had been on the wrong deck when the hull groaned. The tank was spared. The navigators did not do it twice.

outcomes.label: Burn it safe
outcomes.description: Spend what little fuel remains on a clean, controlled passage. Slower, poorer, and everyone lives.
outcomes.log: The navigators refused the gambit and spent the last of the reserve on a wide, safe passage around the well — slower, and it left the tank nearly dry, but the hull came through whole and every soul aboard with it. There would be a reckoning with the fuel later. There was not one now.

title: The Coronal Flare
description: The star throws a flare — a wall of plasma and hard radiation climbing out of the corona straight along the ship's track. There are minutes, not hours. The whole reason the ship is close enough to do its work is the reason the flare can reach it.

outcomes.label: Full shielding, dump the grid to it
outcomes.description: Throw every watt into the radiation shields and ride it out behind them. The work stops; the ship lives.
outcomes.log: The ship poured its whole grid into the shields and hunkered behind them as the flare broke over it — a white roar on every sensor, and then it was past. The reactor was drained to nothing and the survey lost a week's rhythm, and not one soul took a dose that mattered.

outcomes.label: Angle the hull and take it
outcomes.description: No time to fully shield. Turn the ship's thickest armor to the star and endure what gets through.
outcomes.log: The ship turned its armored spine to the star and took the flare on the hull, and the outer decks cooked and the sensors blinded and a score on the sunward side took doses no medic could answer. The survey kept its schedule. The burial detail kept pace with it.

title: The Tempting World
description: A world resolves below, lush past the survey's promise — forests to the poles, seas dense with protein, a growing season the hungry ship could gorge on. And that is exactly the danger: a living biosphere is a living biosphere, and nothing that has fed on it has ever come aboard a ship clean.

outcomes.label: Land and harvest
outcomes.description: Fill the holds from a living world. Take the risk that something living comes up with the harvest.
outcomes.log: The ship gorged on a green world and came away with its holds full and a stowaway in the harvest — a mold, a spore, a something that got into the grow-decks and the wards both before anyone named it. The larder was full for a decade. The ship spent that decade learning to live with what it had carried up the gravity well.

outcomes.label: Skim it sterile from orbit
outcomes.description: No landing. Scoop the upper seas and the pollen-thick air through the sterilizers, take the smaller haul, and keep the ship clean.
outcomes.log: The ship kept its distance and skimmed what it could through the sterilizers — a thinner haul than a landing would have given, and every gram of it dead and safe. The crews watched the green world turn below and did not set foot on it, and the ship sailed on hungry and clean.

title: The Rationed Water
description: The water reclaimers are fouling faster than they can be scoured, and the ship has quietly moved to water rationing — a thing the founders swore would never happen on a closed-loop hull. It is not thirst yet. It is the arithmetic that leads to thirst, and the engineers want a decision before it stops being arithmetic.

outcomes.label: Strip and deep-clean the reclaimers
outcomes.description: Take the water plant apart to the membranes and scour it back to spec. Power, parts, and a thirsty month while it is offline.
outcomes.log: The engineers stripped the water plant to its membranes and scoured it clean over a dry, irritable month, and the loop ran sweet again on the far side of it. The founders' promise held, barely, kept by a generation that had to be reminded it was a promise.

outcomes.label: Hold the ration, nurse it along
outcomes.description: Don't take the plant offline. Keep the ration, run the fouled reclaimers gently, and lose the margin — and the few the thin water hits hardest.
outcomes.log: The ship held its water ration rather than lose the plant for a month, nursing the fouled reclaimers along and burying the handful the thin, hard water took — the old, the young, the already-ailing. The loop kept running. The founders' promise did not, quite, and everyone drank knowing it.

title: The Thin Table
description: The larder will not cover everyone at full ration through to the next harvest, and the arithmetic is on the table in front of the council: cut every share the same and let the whole ship go hungry together, or protect the decks the voyage cannot run without and let the shortfall fall where it costs the ship least — which is to say, on the smallest and least essential of its peoples.

outcomes.label: Share the shortfall equally
outcomes.description: Every deck, every people, every officer takes the same cut. It is harder on the whole ship and it feeds no one better — but no one goes to the wall while their neighbour eats, and a ship that starves together stays a ship.
outcomes.log: The council cut every ration the same, top to bottom, and the whole ship went a little hungry together through to the harvest. It was the harder arithmetic and the leaner year for it — but no people learned that it was the one the ship would spend first, and that knowledge, unspent, was worth more than the food it cost.

outcomes.label: Spare the ship, cut the smallest deck
outcomes.description: The voyage cannot run without its engineers, its farmers, its watch. It can, in a lean year, run without full rations for its smallest and least essential people. Protect the many by cutting the few, and keep the ship working through the shortage.
outcomes.log: The council spared the ship by cutting its smallest deck, and the essential work went on unbroken through the thin year while one people learned, precisely, where it stood on this ship's ledger when the food ran short. The ship stayed working. Whether it stayed whole was a question the smallest people would answer later, in their own time and their own way.

complications.description_add: And this ship has been lean for a generation already — there is no comfortable slack left to trim, no fat on the ration to shave, no reserve to fall back on. Every plate cut now is cut from a body that had long since stopped having anything to spare.
complications.log: The triage fell on a ship with nothing left to give — the reserves long emptied, the people long thinned by the lean years — so the cut a well-fed ship would have shrugged off went straight to the bone here, and the bone was already showing.

title: The Fallow Season
description: The larder is failing on-station, and the ship has exactly as many hands as it has work: every crew turned to the charter's labour is a crew not turned to keeping the ship fed. The founders sent this voyage three centuries to do a thing. This generation, hungry, must decide whether the thing or their own survival comes first this season.

outcomes.label: Turn the crews to survival
outcomes.description: Pull the hands off the charter's work and put them to foraging, patching the grow-decks, wringing food from the worksite by any means. The ship will eat. The tally the founders set will slip, and the mission will arrive a little less complete than it might have — but arrive with its people alive.
outcomes.log: The council turned the work crews from the charter to the larder for a season, and the ship fed itself out of the worksite by improvisation and sweat. The founders' tally slipped — the mission would come home a shade less complete than the dead had dreamed — but it would come home crewed, which the living judged the older promise, and the truer one.

outcomes.label: Hold to the founders' work
outcomes.description: The charter is the reason the ship exists, and a generation does not get to set down a three-century task because a season went lean. Keep the crews on the mission, ration hard, and let the shortage take what it takes — the work comes first, as the founders meant it to.
outcomes.log: The council held the crews to the charter through the lean season and let the shortage bite unanswered, and it bit: the memorial wall grew, and the mission did not slip a day. The founders' work came first, exactly as they had written it, paid for in a generation that starved a little rather than set the task down. Some called it discipline. The hungry, mostly, called it the founders' due, and meant it.

title: The Charted Dearth
description: The navigators bring the council a number, not a crisis: sixty years ahead, on the charted course, lies a long lean stretch — a reach of the dark with nothing to skim, no world to stop at, no relay to beg from, a computed gap between one resupply and the next that the ship will cross on whatever it carries in. It is not an emergency. It is a date. The only question is what the ship does with sixty years of warning.

outcomes.label: Trust to the ship's slack
outcomes.description: Sixty years is a long time to hoard against a stretch the models might have drawn wrong, and the ship has always found a way. Note the dearth in the log, carry on as the voyage is provisioned, and meet the lean reach when it comes with whatever the ship happens to be holding.
outcomes.log: The council noted the charted dearth in the log, judged sixty years too long to hoard against a line on a projection, and carried on as provisioned. The reach was still coming — the arithmetic did not care whether anyone prepared for it — but the ship would meet it on whatever it was holding when the day arrived, and trust, as it always had, to slack and luck.

outcomes.label: Lay in against the day
outcomes.description: A warning sixty years out is a gift a lean ship rarely gets. Spend the good decades building sealed reserves against the charted stretch — grain, volatiles, the dull discipline of putting by while there is plenty — so that when the dearth arrives on its date, the ship arrives with it prepared.
outcomes.log: The council spent the good decades laying in against the charted dearth — sealed vaults filled a little at a time, the dull unglamorous discipline of putting by sixty years before the need — and logged the reserves against a date most of those who stocked them would not live to see. The reach was coming. This time, the ship would be holding more than luck when it arrived.

title: The Dearth Arrives
description: It comes exactly when the navigators said it would, two generations after they said it: the long lean reach of the dark, nothing to skim and nowhere to stop, the computed gap the founders' descendants were warned of and largely forgot. The larder must cover the crossing on what the ship laid in — or did not.

complications.description_add: But the sealed reserves a wiser generation laid in sixty years ago answer now, exactly as they were meant to — the crossing is a hardship, not a catastrophe.
complications.log: The vaults the founders' descendants filled two generations early opened into the dearth and carried the ship across it with room to spare — the foresight of people long dead feeding a crossing they had computed but never seen. The lesson logged itself: the cheapest larder a ship ever fills is the one it fills sixty years early.

outcomes.label: Ration the crossing hard
outcomes.description: Cut every share to the bone and cross the lean reach on discipline — hunger shared out equal and thin, the ship holding its people together through the gap on will and arithmetic.
outcomes.log: The ship rationed the charted crossing to the bone and held its people together through the lean reach on shared hunger and iron discipline. The memorial wall grew; unity held, the way it does when a hardship is borne fairly and foreseen. The ship came out the far side thinner and whole, into a stretch of dark with something to skim again at last.

outcomes.label: Spend down every reserve
outcomes.description: Empty the lockers, burn the margins, sell the future to buy the present — cross the dearth by throwing every reserve the ship has at it and arrive on the far side alive and stripped to nothing.
outcomes.log: The ship crossed the dearth by spending everything it had — the lockers emptied, the margins burned, the reserves that were meant for other emergencies thrown at this one — and came out the far side fed and hollow, alive on nothing, one bad year now from a crisis it had disarmed itself against. It had survived the charted reach. It would spend the next stretch of dark rebuilding what surviving it cost.

title: The Fat Years
description: A generation that has never known a thin plate stands in granaries stacked past anything the founders held — bumper harvest on bumper harvest, the stores swollen half again beyond the launch stock, the reclaimers running sweet, the fields generous. Plenty is its own quiet question, and a fat ship forgets to ask it: what is a surplus for?

outcomes.label: Bank it, and keep the founders' thrift
outcomes.description: A ship that has known no hunger should remember it anyway — in its sealed reserves if not in its bellies. Harden the surplus into deep stores, hold the rations where they stand, and let the fat years pay for a lean one no one alive can yet imagine.
outcomes.log: The council read the swollen granaries not as licence but as warning, and did with the fat years what the founders would have done — sealed the surplus into hardened vaults, kept the plates measured, and taught a generation that had never gone hungry to respect a hunger it had never met. It was an austere answer to abundance, and the kind that keeps a ship alive across centuries it cannot see.

outcomes.label: Ease the rations — let the ship live well
outcomes.description: Discipline is for lean years, and these are not lean. Lengthen the feast-days, open the fuller plates, let a generation know plenty as its birthright rather than a thing to be hoarded against a famine that may never come.
outcomes.log: The ship let itself live well in the fat years — the plates fuller, the feast-days longer, a whole generation raised to know plenty as the natural order rather than a windfall to be feared. They were happy years, and honestly earned, and they quietly unlearned the one skill the founders had drilled deepest: how to go without.

title: The Thinning Decks
description: The muster confirms what the quiet corridors already told everyone: the founders cast off with a thousand souls and more, and the ship now barely fills six hundred berths. Whole decks stand dark and sealed for want of people to walk them. A crew that only ever shrinks must decide how to carry a hull built for a fuller ship.

outcomes.label: Consolidate to the living decks
outcomes.description: Seal the empty quarters, draw the crew inward, and run the ship the smaller thing it has become. It is an admission, and it is also sense — warmth and hands and light spent only where people still are.
outcomes.log: The council sealed the dark decks and drew the dwindled crew into the ship's warm heart, running the hull as the smaller thing it had become. It was efficient and a little mournful, a people admitting its own thinning by the doors it chose to close — but the ones who remained lived closer, and warmer, for it.

outcomes.label: Keep the whole ship lit and crewed
outcomes.description: The founders built this ship entire and meant it walked entire. Keep every deck lit and staffed, however thin the watch — a smaller people refusing to live smaller, holding the founders' ship whole against the day the numbers climb again.
outcomes.log: The council kept the whole ship lit and walked, the watches stretched gossamer-thin across decks built for a fuller crew — an act of stubborn faith that the founders' ship should stay the founders' ship, whatever the muster said. It cost the living rest and reach, and it kept something the sealed-off ship would have quietly surrendered.

title: The Hollow Muster
description: Fewer than half the founding number answer the muster now. The work a thousand hands once shared falls to four hundred, and the arithmetic has stopped being kind: either the ship's ambitions shrink to fit the crew, or the crew is worked past what a life should bear to keep the ship's ambitions whole.

outcomes.label: Mothball what the crew can't tend
outcomes.description: Let machines and darkness hold the parts of the ship the crew can no longer reach. Automate the routine, seal the rest, and accept a hull that is a little more mechanism and a little less community than the founders drew.
outcomes.log: The council handed the ship's unreachable reaches to automation and the dark, a hull kept running more by machine than by crew. It freed the thinned muster from work it could not have done, and it moved the ship a step further from the crewed, living thing the founders launched toward something that merely functioned.

outcomes.label: Drive the remaining crew harder
outcomes.description: Keep it all human, all crewed, all tended by hands — and ask those hands for more. Longer watches, fewer idle days, a people carrying a thousand's work on four hundred backs because it will not let the ship become a machine.
outcomes.log: The council asked the four hundred to carry the thousand's work, and they did — longer watches, shorter rest, a ship kept wholly human on the backs of a crew that would not let it be otherwise. It held the founders' ship as a crewed and living thing, and it wore the living thin to do it.

title: The Last Quarter
description: The crew has fallen to a quarter of the founders' number — a line the old survival manuals ruled in red, below which a generation ship's gene pool and its working hands both turn perilous. The question on the council floor is no longer how the voyage thrives. It is whether the voyage can still be crewed at all.

outcomes.label: Pool everything into a viable core
outcomes.description: Abandon the margins — the culture, the comforts, the redundancies — and pour every remaining soul and store into keeping the core of the ship, and the core of a breeding population, alive to arrive. Survive first; be a people later.
outcomes.log: The council stripped the voyage to its viable core — the arts let go, the comforts closed, every hand and calorie bent to the one task of arriving crewed at all. It was a huddle where a society had been, and it kept the ember of the crossing alive past a threshold the founders' manuals had marked as the end of hope.

outcomes.label: Hold the founders' way of life even now
outcomes.description: A crew that saves itself by ceasing to be itself has not really saved anything. Keep the festivals, the schools, the dignities — live as people to the end of the odds rather than survive as cargo — and trust that a whole people is worth more, even now, than a larger frightened one.
outcomes.log: The council refused to trade the ship's soul for its odds, keeping the festivals and the schools and the ordinary dignities even as the muster fell into the manuals' red. It was a gamble with arithmetic the founders would have called reckless, and it meant that whoever arrived, or didn't, did so as people rather than as frightened cargo.

title: The Gilded Hunger
description: The treasury has never been fatter and the larder never thinner — a fortune banked from good contracts sits idle in the ledgers while the granaries run to dust, and the nearest port where a credit might buy a loaf is lightyears and lifetimes off. The oldest lesson of the deep dark comes due again in the empty mess halls: out here, you cannot eat money.

outcomes.label: Endure it — wealth is worthless out here
outcomes.description: Ration to the bone and bear the loss. The founders knew what the fat ledgers make it easy to forget: between the ports, a hull's only true wealth is what grows in its own decks. Hold the treasury for the day it means something again, and meet the famine the way people always have — together, and short.
outcomes.log: The council let the fortune sit untouched and met the hunger on the founders' terms — rationed to the bone, borne together, the dead counted and the treasury kept whole for a port generations off. It was the hard old wisdom of the dark relearned, that a hull between waystations is only ever as rich as its own fields, and the ledgers full of useless credits were a bitter comfort in the empty mess halls.

outcomes.label: Burn the reserve chasing a resupply
outcomes.description: Spend the fuel to reach the faint chance — a charted rock, a rumored waystation, anywhere the money might finally buy a hold of grain. It is a gamble with the voyage's own margin, but a full treasury is no use to a crew that starves before it can spend it.
outcomes.log: The council burned deep into the fuel reserve and cracked the treasury open, chasing a waystation the charts only half-promised — and found it, barely, a grudging trader who took the ship's money and gave grain the crew fell upon. The famine broke. The tank ran perilous and the coffers came down hard, and the ship learned that a fortune is worth exactly the fuel it takes to reach a place to spend it.

title: The Well-Stocked Doldrums
description: There is a strange, fat helplessness aboard. The holds are full — a bumper century of harvests, the granaries brimming — and the fuel tank is all but dry, the ship coasting on the last of its reaction mass through a dead reach of the dark with no push left in it. A crew with everything to eat and no way to move, becalmed and comfortable and going nowhere.

outcomes.label: Conserve the fuel and wait it out
outcomes.description: There is food to spare and no reason to panic. Cut the drive to a whisper, live off the full larder, and let patience and a passing current do what the empty tank cannot. The ship loses time it cannot spend, but a fed crew can afford to wait where a hungry one cannot.
outcomes.log: The council cut the drive to nothing and let the ship drift the dead reach on its brimming stores, a fed and patient crew waiting out the doldrums no fuel could break. It cost the voyage long idle years it would rather have spent, and it cost nothing that mattered, because a ship with a full larder and its people calm can afford to wait for the dark to move it when it cannot move itself.

outcomes.label: Spend the last fuel to break free
outcomes.description: Doldrums have a way of becoming graves. Burn what fuel remains in one hard push to claw back into a live current while the drive still answers, and trust the full holds to carry a crew that will, after this, have almost no reaction mass at all.
outcomes.log: The council spent nearly the last of the fuel in one hard burn and clawed the ship back into a living current before the dead reach could close around it for good. It left the tank a whisper from empty and the hull the worse for the strain, and it kept the voyage a voyage rather than a well-fed tomb drifting fat and purposeless in the deep.

title: The Long Hunger
description: This is no famine year. The stores have run thin for fifteen years and more — a whole cohort raised to adulthood having never once seen a full granary, hunger settled from crisis into climate, the ordinary background condition of a life aboard. The children of the lean years do not fear the hunger the way their elders did; they simply do not know anything else. A ship that has been hungry for a generation must decide whether that is a thing to end or a thing to become.

outcomes.label: Break it, whatever it costs
outcomes.description: Refuse to let hunger become the ship's nature. Throw every idle hand at the grow-decks, retool whole compartments to hydroponics, spend the reserves on the biggest resupply push the ship can mount — end the long lean by main force, and teach the lean generation that scarcity is a problem to be solved and not a fate to be borne.
outcomes.log: The council refused to let the hunger become the ship, and broke the long lean by sheer effort — decks retooled to grow-beds, reserves poured into one great resupply push, every idle hand bent to the fields until the granaries answered at last. It cost the treasury and the stores dear, and it taught the lean generation the thing their elders feared they would forget: that hunger is an enemy to be beaten, not a weather to be endured.

outcomes.label: Make the ship a people of the lean
outcomes.description: A ship cannot always outfight the dark. Stop spending the treasury against the hunger and instead adapt to it — ration as a way of life, breed thrift into every rite and every recipe, make a virtue and an identity of going without. A people shaped by scarcity is a hardy people, even if it is a hungry one.
outcomes.log: The council stopped fighting the hunger and began, instead, to belong to it — ration woven into rite, thrift bred into the young until going without was less a hardship than a heritage. The ship that came out of the long lean was a hardier and a hungrier thing than the founders launched, a people who had made scarcity into character, and who would look on any future plenty with the deep suspicion of those who have learned not to trust a full plate.

title: The Soft Generation
description: The granaries have stood full for as long as anyone under forty can remember. A whole generation has come to adulthood having never seen a measured plate, never counted a ration, never once believed the food might run out — plenty is not good fortune to them but simply the way of things, the natural order of a well-run ship. Their elders, who remember otherwise, watch them with an unease they cannot quite justify: what does a people do, when the dark finally tests it, that has never learned how to go without?

outcomes.label: Drill the discipline they lack
outcomes.description: Manufacture the hardship the good years have spared them. Symbolic fasts, ration drills, the old lean recipes kept on the calendar though the larder groans — teach a plenty-raised generation, against its will and its comfort, the one skill a long voyage cannot do without: how to be hungry on purpose, so that being hungry by necessity does not break them.
outcomes.log: The council drilled scarcity into a generation that had never met it — fasts and ration exercises and the lean old recipes kept on the calendar amid a plenty that made them feel absurd. The young resented it as a manufactured misery, which it was, and carried out of it the one thing the fat years could not give them: the muscle-memory of going without, banked against a dark that had not tested them yet.

outcomes.label: Let them have their birthright
outcomes.description: The founders crossed the dark so their heirs would not have to fear it. Who are the living to deny the young the very abundance the whole voyage was for? Let them enjoy the plenty as the founders intended, unburdened by a hunger that is, for now, only a story — and trust that when the dark comes they will rise to it as their elders did.
outcomes.log: The council let the young have the plenty their elders had crossed the dark to give them, unburdened by a fear that was only a story to them now. They were happier for it, and honestly so, and they grew up with no more idea of hunger than a fish has of drowning — a softness banked against a winter that, when it came, would ask them for a thing they had never once been made to learn.

title: The Distrustful Plenty
description: The stores are full again, fuller than they have been in living memory — and a ship that made scarcity into its very identity does not know what to do with them. This is a people bred to thrift, that wove rationing into rite and taught its children to fear a full plate, and now the full plates have come and the fear has not left with the hunger. The young hoard against a famine that is over. The old distrust the abundance as a trick the dark is playing. Plenty has arrived, and the ship must decide whether it is even allowed to enjoy it.

outcomes.label: Trust nothing — keep the lean ways
outcomes.description: The hunger taught the ship a lesson written in the bodies of the dead: the dark gives and the dark takes, and a people that forgets the taking dies of the next famine it did not believe in. Keep the rationing, keep the thrift, bank every surplus grain against the lean years that always, always come back. Let the plenty make the ship safe, not soft.
outcomes.log: The ship refused to trust its own good fortune, keeping the lean discipline the long hunger had bred into it — the surplus banked and sealed rather than enjoyed, the rationing held though the larder overflowed. It was a hoarder's caution that wasted much of the plenty, and it was the caution of a people who had counted the dead of a famine they had not believed in, and did not intend to count them twice.

outcomes.label: Let the young unlearn the fear
outcomes.description: The thrift was a survival, not a scripture. The young who never knew the long hunger see no sense in starving amid plenty, and they are not wrong — a fear kept past its cause is just a wound worn as a virtue. Let the lean identity soften, let the plenty be plenty, and let a people that suffered enough finally be allowed to stop.
outcomes.log: The ship let its hard-won thrift soften at last, the young unlearning a fear whose cause was a generation dead, the lean rites quietly retired amid a plenty that made them look less like wisdom than like a wound worn too long as a virtue. Something austere and defining went out of the ship with the fear, and something lighter came in — a people that had suffered enough, finally allowing itself to stop.

title: The Kindness Returned
description: The larder is thin and the ship is bracing to ration hard — and then, unhailed and unasked, a stranger's hull matches course and offers to share what it has. Its captain explains, almost apologetically, that word of this ship travels: that here is a vessel known to help others in exactly this strait, and that the dark keeps a longer memory than anyone credits it with. The mercy the ship spent generations building, one small choice at a time, has come back around to it at the moment it needs mercy itself.

outcomes.label: Accept it gladly, and remember the debt
outcomes.description: Take the aid with an open hand and a grateful heart, and file the debt where the ship files all such things — not as a ledger entry to be repaid but as a bond to be carried forward, one more strand in the web of mercy that just, for once, held the ship up instead of others. A people that gives freely and receives freely is a people the dark looks after.
outcomes.log: The ship took the stranger's aid gladly and gratefully, and the famine broke before it had properly begun — the mercy of generations returning, unbidden, at the hour of need. The crew learned a thing that no ration drill teaches: that a name for kindness is the one larder the dark cannot empty, and that a people generous in the good years is a people someone comes for in the lean ones.

outcomes.label: Accept, but insist on paying fair
outcomes.description: Gratitude is one thing and dependence another; the ship's pride does not sit easy under a debt it cannot see the end of. Take the aid, break the famine, and press hard credit on the stranger in return — not to diminish the kindness but to keep the ship a giver and not a taker, even in the strait where it must, this once, receive.
outcomes.log: The ship took the aid and paid for it in hard credit, breaking the famine and keeping its pride intact — a giver even in the hour it had to receive. The stranger shrugged and took the payment, a little bemused, and the ship sailed on fed and square with the dark, having declined even in extremity to be the sort of hull that simply takes.

title: The Famine Faced Alone
description: The larder is failing and the ship does the thing any ship does: it hails the dark for aid. And the dark, this time, does not answer. The hulls in range go quiet, or answer only to decline; the waystations find reasons; word has traveled, and the word is that this is a ship that takes and does not give, that left castaways to the void and cut its own weak to spare its strong, and that a hull like that can face its lean years the way it made others face theirs — alone. The reputation the ship built one hard choice at a time has become a debt, and it has come due at the worst hour there is.

outcomes.label: Endure it alone, and count the cost
outcomes.description: There is no one coming, and there is a certain grim clarity in knowing it. Ration to the bone, bury the dead, and bear the famine on the ship's own discipline and nothing else — and let the crew draw what solidarity it can from a hardship that is, at least, entirely theirs, earned by their own hand and borne by it too.
outcomes.log: No one came, and the ship endured its famine the way it had made others endure theirs — alone, on its own hard discipline, burying its dead and asking nothing of a dark that had nothing to offer a hull like it. There was a bleak solidarity in it, a people bearing a hardship earned entirely by its own hand, and there was the cold arithmetic of a reputation come due: that the ship which gives nothing is given nothing, at exactly the hour it has nothing itself.

outcomes.label: Take what you need by force
outcomes.description: If the dark will not help a feared ship, let it fear the ship properly. There is a weaker hull in range with stores it cannot defend; take them. The ship is already known for the hard thing done without flinching — this is only one more, and a hungry crew is not the crew to develop a conscience it never had when it was fed.
outcomes.log: The ship took what it needed from a hull too weak to keep it, breaking its famine on someone else's stores and deepening, one more notch, the fearsome name that had left it with no gentler option. It was efficient and it was a prophecy fulfilling itself — a ship that no one would help becoming, hunger by hunger, a ship no one dared refuse — and the dark's memory, already cold toward it, grew colder by exactly the width of the theft.
